entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: on either horn, no adi, no beginning, is found

entities.name: ādi
entities.cite: no adi, no beginning, is found

entities.name: nityatā
entities.cite: Bhartrhari declines to settle whether words are nitya or krtaka

entities.name: kṛtakatvā
entities.cite: Bhartrhari declines to settle whether words are nitya or krtaka

entities.name: prāṇin
entities.cite: The analogy to praninam carries the argument

entities.name: vyavasthā
entities.cite: the settled order in which items stand

entities.name: anādinidhanā
entities.cite: their beginninglessness is perfectly compatible with each member being made

axioms.name: anupalabdha_adi
axioms.cite: on either horn, no adi, no beginning, is found

axioms.name: iva
axioms.cite: The analogy to praninam carries the argument

axioms.name: nityata
axioms.cite: what is eternal is not necessarily the item but the settled order in which items stand

axioms.name: anadinidhana
axioms.cite: the series of them has no first term, and their beginninglessness is perfectly compatible with each member being made

denials.name: adi
denials.cite: no adi, no beginning, is found

claims.name: anupalabdha_adi
claims.cite: no beginning of them is found

claims.name: nityata
claims.cite: this arrangement is what is called eternality

rejected_readings.label: individual_eternality
rejected_readings.rendering: Individual words possess eternality as an intrinsic metaphysical property.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's concessive opening ('whether they are eternal or made') explicitly declines any commitment to individual eternality. Its thesis is vyavastha-nityata (eternality of arrangement), not the eternality of words themselves. This reading commits to precisely what the verse brackets.

rejected_readings.label: words_have_origin
rejected_readings.rendering: Words, whether eternal or made, possess an identifiable beginning or origin point.
rejected_readings.why: Directly contradicts the core logical claim: on both horns of the disjunction (nitya or krtaka), no adi, no beginning, is found for them. This is the denial axis of the verse.

notes: The verse proposes vyavastha-nityata (eternality of arrangement) as a weaker, more defensible thesis than individual eternality, preserving the linguistic order's beginninglessness without winning the metaphysical debate about word-substance. The analogy with pranin (living beings)—each produced, yet the series has no first member—anchors the argument. All six sorts fit: sabda and pranin are linguisticItem and power; adi, nityata, krtakatva, anadinidhana are properties; vyavastha is manifestation.
